edus.text: Conservatives are up in arms over the New York Times ' s latest hire :
a tech writer
named Sarah Jeong
whom they allege to be racist against white people .
Jeong ,
who currently works at the Media site The Verge ,
was hired by the Times editorial board
to work on technology issues .
On Thursday ,
shortly after the hire was announced ,
conservative publications dug up old tweets of hers
containing statements like
" white men are bullshit "
and " oh man it ' s kind of sick how much joy I get out
of being cruel to old white men . "
The campaign
to use these tweets
to get her fired
seems to have failed .
The Times issued a statement
saying
that Jeong had meant these tweets satirically
- a parody of the hate
she has received online as an Asian woman -
and that they were standing by her .
But to some conservatives , like National Review ' s David French and New York magazine ' s Andrew Sullivan , Jeong ' s tweets are bigger than her :
They reveal a rot in the progressive movement ,
that " social justice warriors " have become totally okay with racism
so long as it ' s directed at white people .
Both French and Sullivan singled out a tweet
I had sent
defending Jeong
- " a lot of people on the internet today confusing the expressive way
anti-racists and minorities talk about ' white people '
with actual race-based hatred , for some unfathomable reason " -
as an exemplar of the problem .
Sullivan explains :
I don ' t think
the New York Times should fire her
- in part because they largely share her views on race , gender , and oppression .
Their entire hiring and editorial process is based on them .
In their mind , Jeong was merely caught defending herself .
As writer Zack Beauchamp put it :
" A lot of people on the internet today
[ are ] confusing the expressive way
antiracists and minorities talk about ' white people '
with actual race-based hatred , for some unfathomable reason.
" I have to say
that word " expressive " made me chuckle out loud .
( But would Beauchamp ,
I wonder ,
feel the same way
if anti-racists talked about Jews in the same manner
Jeong talks about whites ?
Aren ' t Jews included in the category of whites ? )
I have a lot of respect for Andrew
- he gave me my first job in journalism ,
and I ' ve been over to his DC condo more times
than I can count -
but I think
he has a serious blind spot
when it comes to race ,
and the analogy to Jews here helps reveal it .
Both his piece and French ' s misunderstand what racism is and how the so-called " social justice left " approaches the world
and the anti-Jeong vitriol
you ' ve seen from the right
speaks more to its failings on race
than it does anything about Jeong .
The basic thrust of both Sullivan and French ' s argument is that
if you subbed in any group other than " white people " for what Jeong wrote ,
then it would be obviously offensive .
" # cancelblackpeople probably wouldn ' t fly at the New York Times ,
would it ? "
Sullivan asks , rhetorically .
The only reason
lefties aren ' t offended by this obvious race-based hatred ,
the argument goes ,
is that they see the world entirely through the lens of power .
Since whites as a class have it ,
minorities by definition can not harbor racist attitudes toward them .
" It is simply false to excuse anti-white racism on the grounds
that people of color lack power , "
French writes .
" But this argument confuses the gravity of an offense with the existence of the offense .
A powerless person ' s hate may not harm the powerful ,
but it is still hate . "
The problem here , though , is assuming that Jeong ' s words were meant literally :
that
when Jeong wrote " # cancelwhitepeople , " for example ,
she was literally calling for white genocide .
Or
when she said
" white men are bullshit , "
she meant
each and every white man is the human equivalent of bull feces .
This is expressly Sullivan ' s position :
He calls her language " eliminationist , " a term
most commonly used
to describe Nazi rhetoric
referring to Jews during the Holocaust .
To anyone
who ' s even passingly familiar with the way
the social justice left talks ,
this is just clearly untrue .
" White people " is a shorthand in these communities , one
that ' s used
to capture the way
that many whites still act in clueless and/or racist ways .
It ' s typically used satirically and hyperbolically
to emphasize how white people continue to benefit
( even unknowingly )
from their skin color ,
or to point out the ways
in which a power structure
that favors white people
continues to exist .
I get that white people
who aren ' t familiar
might find this discomforting .
Sullivan think
t ' s unacceptable on an " an open-forum website like Twitter. "
ut the reality is that Twitter is where these conversations between people of color are taking place ,
nd
given the 280-character limit )
t ' s a lot easier to use the kind of " white people " shorthand
ather than adding endless qualifications
" a certain kind of white person , definitely Not All White People , " is pretty lengthy ) .
he sentence
white people run America " may use most of the same words
s " Jews run America , "
ut the former is mostly true
hile the latter is an anti-Semitic conspiracy theory
hat makes these quasi-satirical generalizations about " white people " different from actual racism is , yes , the underlying power structure in American society .
here is no sense of threat
ssociated with Jeong making a joke
bout how white people have dog-like opinions .
ut
hen white people have said the same about minorities ,
t has historically been a pretext for violence or justification for exclusionary politics .
his is why Sullivan ' s use of " eliminationist "
o describe Jeong ' s words
s , to my mind , particularly ill-chosen .
liminationist language , in the way
t ' s used by scholars of genocide and racial oppression ,
s used as justification for concrete actions
the Holocaust is the textbook example ,
nd the Rwandan genocide is another clear one .
ut the very idea
hat Sarah Jeong ' s tweets reveal
er desire
o set up concentration camps for whites
s laughable .
his is , incidentally , why you hear a lot of people on the social left say
here ' s no such thing as " reverse racism. "
e interpret language through social context .
ecause of the way
acial power structures are set up ,
he same set of words mean very different things
hen you swap out " white people " for " black people , " " Asians , " " Jews , " etc .
he phrase might be racist in one context , in the sense
f conveying actual racial animus ,
ut not in another .
his meaning of the phrase " white people " is obvious to people
ho have been listening to these social media conversations ,
r even from even a cursory search of Twitter for the term .
' ve copied a few examples below :
don ' t see how Sarah ' s tweets are much different
han how a lot of influential younger POC have been talking on Twitter these last few years .
t just seems like the standard here is that you can ' t say it
hile working at the New York Times .
Matt Pearce
@ mattdpearce )
ugust 2 , 2018
hite people finding out
hat we talk about white people as a means
o vent about and survive the whiteness
e 're forced to experience
s ,
o put it one way ,
ever boring .
Studio Glibly
@ NoTotally )
ugust 3 , 2018
he other thing
'd note
s that
f you 're a white person
ho 'd never heard POC talk about white people before the advent of social media ,
t probably means that no POC , even your friends , have truly trusted you .
Studio Glibly
@ NoTotally )
ugust 3 , 2018
hite people :
omes to America ,
laims it ,
icks out the natives forcibly
hich thousands of were killed in this process
hite people :
ets make immigrants illegal

hite people will shame you
nd then copy you
nd take credit .
ake up

eong ' s tweets , in context , clearly fit this type of rhetoric .
hen she writes
dumbass fucking white people
arking up the internet with their opinions like dogs
issing on fire hydrants , "
he is not ,
s Sullivan accuses her of doing ,
equat
ing whites ]
ith animals. "
ather , she is commenting on the ubiquity of
often uniformed )
hite opinion on social media
a way
f pointing out how nonwhite voices often don ' t appear or get drowned out in social media discourse .
gain , this is obvious to people
ho are steeped in the kind of online communities
eong spends time in .
ut what happened ,
s my colleague Aja Romano notes ,
s that a few alt-right websites pulled out Jeong ' s tweets in a deliberate attempt
o hurt her career and reputation .
hat ' s actually happening here is a racist movement , the alt-right ,
rying to damage a left-wing woman of color
and mainstream conservatives are furthering their narrative .
don ' t necessarily blame Sullivan and French
or not hanging out on Social Justice Twitter ,
here their views would be ... unwelcome ,
o say the least .
hat I do blame them for is joining an alt-right pile-on
ithout trying to understand where their left-wing intellectual opponents are coming from and what they ' re talking about .
n his column , Sullivan challenges me , specifically , to explain
ow I would feel
f Jeong were tweeting about Jews rather than whites .
Would Beauchamp ,
wonder ,
eel the same way
f anti-racists talked about Jews in the same manner
eong talks about whites ?
ren ' t Jews included in the category of whites ? "
e asks .
he example ,
assume ,
s chosen deliberately :
ndrew knows
' m Jewish
nd sensitive to the real problem of anti-Semitism on the left .
o this seems like it should be a hard example for me , as a Jeong defender .
ut it ' s not at all .
n fact , it makes the distinction between what Jeong is doing even stronger .
irst of all , not all Jews are white .
econd , even for those Jews
ho are ,
ur inclusion in the category of whiteness is historically contingent .
or the modern alt-right
the people
oing after Jeong -
ews don ' t actually count as white .
eading alt-right thinker Richard Spencer has said
hat in his ideal world , Jews would be expelled from the United States .
ews are not marginalized in the exact same way
hat other minority groups are
on average , Jews are wealthier
hile black people are poorer -
ut there is deep-seated anti-Semitism in the United States and
even more so )
n other Western countries .
hen you talk about " Jews " as a group ,
ou aren ' t just talking about a specific kind of white person ;
ou ' re talking about a historically marginalized group ,
ne
hat has experienced the consequences of actual eliminationist rhetoric firsthand .
nd this is what the conservative critiques of Jeong
even the good-faith ones , like Sullivan ' s and French ' s -
iss entirely .
hen you talk about race and identity ,
he context
n which you ' re operating
s absolutely inescapable .
he sentence " white people run America " may use most of the same words
s " Jews run America , "
ut the former is mostly true
hile the latter is an anti-Semitic conspiracy theory .
istorical structures of oppression , along race and other identity lines , shape the way
mericans see the world deeply and profoundly .
verything
hat ' s said about minority groups
s interpreted through the weight of these expectations .
ne study found
hat children start thinking in racist terms
hen they ' re about 8 years old .
t ' s absurd to pretend ,
iven centuries of racialized oppression ,
hat the phrases " white people " and " black people " can be swapped in a sentence
ithout profoundly changing the meaning .
his is indicative of a broader failing in the conservative movement .
he weight of history is typically minimized ,
ith the success or failure of minority groups being seen in colorblind , individualized terms .
f black people aren ' t as wealthy as whites ,
t ' s not the legacy of slavery and Jim Crow and redlining ;
t ' s because they ' re lazy , or addicted to welfare ,
r
as Sullivan has posited )
imply genetically less likely to be intelligent .
here has to be some explanation other than the fact
hat everything in our society is structured around a longstanding racial caste system , one
hat persists
ven after the laws have been made on-face colorblind .
his isn ' t a mere matter
f there being a power differential between whites and various minority groups ,
s French would have it .
t ' s about the reasons
here ' s a power differential ,
nd how those reasons shape even the very meaning of the words
e speak .
blindness to history , and the patterns
et by people long dead ,
s a characteristic of conservative race thinking
and a somewhat ironic one ,
iven conservative reverence for tradition ) .
want to close on some more recent history :
similar debate
hat happened online in 2014 .
he issue then was gender .
number of feminist writers had a habit
f writing about " men " on social media without qualification like " most " or " the majority of. "
his was partly for simplicity ' s sake ,
nd partly to point out how widespread a lot of sexist practices are .
his led to a lot of responses from men
hey didn ' t know ,
aying something along the lines of
not all men are sexist ,
nd you ' re the real sexist
or saying
hey are. "
ational Review , French ' s publication , published an entire column
aking a basically similar argument .
he feminist writers responded
hat this was a distraction .
t was obvious they weren ' t talking about literally every man in context ,
nd it was clear these men were butting in on conversations about gender
o derail them with a pointlessly persnickety objection
ather than dealing with the substantive conversation about sexism .
o the feminist writers responded
y turning the phrase " not all men " into a point of mockery ,
sing it as an example of men
idetracking feminist arguments
hat made them uncomfortable .
he feminists won this argument ;
oday , feminists still complain about " men , "
nd " not all men " is mostly used as a punchline rather than a serious argument .
ut the conservative responses to Jeong boil down , essentially , to the same thing :
hey ' re saying
not all white people " are bad
nd Jeong is a racist
or implying
hat they are .
y guess is ,
few years down the road , we ' ll remember the Jeong episode in roughly the same way
e remember the # NotAllMen controversy today .
